project_overview.project_name: Afghanistan Water, Sanitation, Hygiene and Institutional Support Project (A-WASH)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve access to and quality of water supply in selected cities and to strengthen the capacity of AUWSSC to deliver sustainable services in order to contribute to national efforts to manage COVID-19 and other disasters
project_overview.total_financing: US$200 million (US$50 million IDA Grant + US$150 million ARTF)
project_overview.beneficiaries: 1.95 million Afghans directly, including 1.15 million current customers in Kabul, Herat, and Kandahar, plus up to 800,000 additional people in Kandahar through expanded access

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Afghanistan is a fragile and conflict-affected state with ongoing active conflict despite peace agreement with Taliban, high security risks, and unstable political situation

key_components.component_name: Component 1: COVID-19 Emergency Relief and Recovery
key_components.description: Support for AUWSSC to maintain vital water services during COVID-19 pandemic through financing of treatment chemicals, rehabilitation of priority water facilities (wells, pipes, pumps, generators, chlorine dosing equipment), and emergency water connections for high-risk areas and health facilities
key_components.financing: US$30 million (US$5 million IDA + US$25 million ARTF)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Sector Reform, Institutional Strengthening, and Capacity Building
key_components.description: Technical assistance for sector reform, strategic planning, financial and technical performance improvements, social accountability, citizen engagement, grievance redress mechanism, and preparation of feasibility studies for second project

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Enabling Inclusive Access to Safe Water
key_components.description: Infrastructure investments in Kandahar including bulk transmission pipeline from Dahla Dam (30 km), water treatment plant (Phase 1: 125,000 m³/day capacity), service reservoirs, booster pumping stations, and distribution network expansion (80,000 connections, 1,000 standpipes)
key_components.financing: US$160 million (US$40 million IDA + US$120 million ARTF)

key_components.description: Support for Project Coordination Team and Project Implementation Units in three cities, including financial management, procurement, environmental and social safeguards, monitoring and evaluation
Contingency mechanism to respond to eligible crises or emergencies by reallocating funds from other components

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Afghanistan Urban Water Supply and Sewerage Corporation (AUWSSC), with Project Implementation Units (PIUs) in Kabul, Kandahar, and Herat Strategic Business Units (SBUs), and Project Coordination Team (PCT) at AUWSSC headquarters
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Coordination Committee (PCC) chaired by Ministry of Finance with representatives from AUWSSC, NWARA, municipalities, MAIL, MRRD, and donors; AUWSSC-Donors Coordination (ADC) group including World Bank, USAID, KfW, GIZ, ADB, JICA
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF; Standard Procurement Documents for open international competition; Model Procurement Documents for national open competition with six NPP conditions; STEP system for tracking; prior review thresholds: Works US$5M, Goods/IT/Non-consulting US$1.5M, Consulting firms US$0.5M, Individual consultants US$0.2M
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: AUWSSC PCT and SBU PIUs responsible for continuous M&E; dedicated M&E Specialist at PCT; semi-annual reports to Bank; construction supervision monthly reports; annual self-evaluation; benchmarking system to collect data on key indicators; independent surveys for baseline, midterm, and endline; citizen satisfaction surveys

identified_risks.risk_description: Afghanistan's fragile and unstable political situation with ongoing conflict despite peace agreement; AUWSSC vulnerable to political capture, insecurity, and instability; risks from changing political leadership, high staff turnover, difficulties accessing field sites, and political resistance to reforms like tariff increases
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Frequent high-level dialogue with GoIRA and key donors; emphasis on locally-based implementing agency staff backed by AUWSSC and World Bank staff in Kabul; early detection and appropriate reaction to political challenges

identified_risks.risk_description: Afghanistan's fragile macroeconomic outlook with low growth, uncertain fiscal resources, high security expenditures; COVID-19 crisis causing severe economic contraction (5.5-7.4% in 2020), declining revenues, limited grant support; poverty expected to spike from 55% to 72% in 2020
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project includes critical expenditures expected to be prioritized even if macroeconomic risks materialize; close continuous dialogue to ensure continued prioritization

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Weak institutional capacity in AUWSSC and NWARA with limited recent experience with World Bank investment projects; weak financial management systems, limited procurement experience, poor asset management, weak technical capacity
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establishment of capable PIUs and PCT; Project Coordination Committee for support and coordination; close supervision by World Bank task team based in Kabul; robust technical assistance and training; capacity building support to AUWSSC and SBUs staff

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak financial transparency and accountability; weak systems and capacity for accounting, financial reporting, and asset management; history of audit disclaimers; weak internal controls; manual processes with no segregation of duties; poor billing and collection systems
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Enhanced Fiduciary Measures with TPMA monitoring; Financial Management Manual compliance; quarterly SOE verification by TPMA; semi-annual internal audits; annual external audits by Supreme Audit Office; hiring of FM Specialists; development and implementation of financial management roadmap; mandatory direct payments for international procurement

identified_risks.risk_description: Overlapping sector responsibilities leading to inadequate strategies and policies; inadequate sector governance; unfunded or financially unrealistic strategies; key policies (including tariffs) not designed for financial sustainability; unpredictable and variable sector funding
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Direct technical assistance to stakeholders on utility governance, strategy, and tariff setting; support for sector reform and strategic planning under Component 2

identified_risks.risk_description: Lack of security across Afghanistan poses significant challenges to project implementation; Bank teams unable to conduct site visits; risk of deterioration in security situation diverting government capacity, causing delays or halting implementation; security concerns impeding technical assistance and reform implementation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Development of security risk management system; building consensus and support with local communities and political stakeholders through participatory planning; job creation planning; contingency planning for conflict-related disruptions; locally-based PIU with autonomy to operate efficiently

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial environmental and social risks from construction activities; low capacity of AUWSSC to manage E&S standards; lack of effective wastewater collection and disposal systems in Kandahar; COVID-19 related risks including improper social distancing, poor labor camp management, waste disposal; GBV moderate risk; child labor and forced labor concerns; land acquisition and resettlement impacts
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Preparation of ESMF, ESIA, ESMP, Resettlement Framework, Stakeholder Engagement Plan, Labor Management Procedures; Environmental and Social Commitment Plan; recruitment of qualified environmental and social specialists; capacity building plan; GRM establishment; GBV action plan; adherence to WHO COVID-19 guidelines; chance find procedures for cultural heritage

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of disruption from coordination with NWARA PIU for Dahla Dam project; potential withdrawal of partners due to security or other concerns; need for significant coordination with multiple stakeholders
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close dialogue with stakeholders through ADC Group and PCC; coordination with USAID and ADB on complementary investments; harmonized capacity building roadmap with all key donors

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
OP 7.50: Projects on International Waterways (Arghandab River, Harirud River, Kabul River)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Two-tier GRM system to be established by AUWSSC including multiple channels for complaints, registration in log, MIS-based database, published service standards for acknowledgement and response, transparency about procedures, mediation and appeal options; training for staff and beneficiaries; Grievance Redressal Committees at project sites; proper registration system for tracking and review
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender assessment conducted; significant gender gaps identified at both institutional and community levels; women underrepresented in AUWSSC (less than 10% of staff, 3% in leadership); project will support gender strategy development, targeted technical and managerial training for female staff, recruitment of women engineers through university links, paid internship program with 25% female target, improved working conditions (daycare, sanitation facilities, harassment-free environment), women's participation in all trainings, community consultations with women, pictorial manuals for female beneficiaries, GBV action plan development
vulnerable_groups: Poor and vulnerable households (55% poverty rate in 2017, expected to spike to 72% in 2020)
Women and girls (primary water users and managers, limited access to information)
Displaced persons and returnees from Iran
Informal water vendors and tanker truck operators (potential livelihood impacts)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project designed with conflict sensitivity in mind; emphasis on simplicity of design with limited components and sites to minimize demands on weak institutions; locally-based PIU with autonomy to operate efficiently; continuous consensus building with government, beneficiaries, and stakeholders; participatory planning and job creation to build support; contingency budgeting for security uncertainties; engagement with local communities and political stakeholders to defuse resistance; GRM to address grievances that could escalate conflicts; communication campaigns to build understanding and support
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan is a fragile and conflict-affected state with: ongoing active conflict despite peace agreement; unstable political situation with power-sharing implementation risks; high poverty (55% in 2017, projected 72% in 2020); weak institutions and governance; limited fiscal resources (grants equal 43% of GDP); high security expenditures; COVID-19 pandemic causing severe economic contraction; weak human capital (HCI of 0.4); high exposure to shocks (conflict, droughts, floods); limited access to basic services; weak sector institutions with overlapping mandates; AUWSSC vulnerable to political capture and instability
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: High security risk rating; Bank teams unable to conduct site visits; risk of deterioration in security situation; need for security risk management system; earlier rehabilitation of dam inlet works (2014-15) completed without security incidents but current situation more challenging; security concerns could impede technical assistance and reform implementation; need for local presence and autonomy of PIU to operate in challenging security environment; contingency planning for conflict-related disruptions; job creation and community engagement to build support and reduce conflict risk
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Project context includes influx of hundreds of thousands of people returning to Afghanistan from Iran due to COVID-19, exacerbating pandemic vulnerability; project supports returnees and vulnerable populations through improved water and sanitation access; potential livelihood impacts on informal water vendors and tanker truck operators requiring social outreach and transition support; land acquisition and resettlement impacts possible from water network construction requiring Resettlement Framework and mitigation measures
